channel.title: NYT > Books > Book Review
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: Book Review: ‘Checkmate,’ by Ben Mezrich
item.description: The story at the heart of “Checkmate” is a good one, but Ben Mezrich has hustled it into print too quickly.
item.dc:creator: Dylan Loeb McClain
item.category: Checkmate: Genius, Lies, Ambition, and the Biggest Scandal in Chess (Book)
item.media:description: Norway’s Magnus Carlsen, pictured here, made waves in the chess world when he accused Hans Niemann, a brash young American, of cheating.

item.title: 11 Books That Capture the Swoon-Worthy Drama of Wedding Season
item.description: These books dig into the thrilling, ugly and swoon-worthy drama of a happy couple’s big day.
item.dc:creator: Jenny Singer

item.title: The Best Audiobooks of 2026 (So Far)
item.description: The year is nearly halfway over. Here’s what we’ve been listening to.
item.dc:creator: Lauren Christensen
item.category: A Hymn to Life: Shame Has to Change Sides (Book)
Days of Love and Rage: A Story of Ordinary People Forging a Revolution (Book)
London Falling: A Mysterious Death in a Gilded City and a Family's Search for Truth (Book)

item.title: Book Review: ‘Whistler,’ by Ann Patchett
item.description: In “Whistler,” a surprise encounter at the Met changes the course of two lives.
item.dc:creator: Helen Schulman

item.title: Books Our Editors Loved This Week
item.description: Reading recommendations from critics and editors at The New York Times.
item.category: The Man Who Read Everything: The Literary Letters of Harold Bloom (Book)

item.title: Alan Gribben, Twain Scholar Who Excised Slur From ‘Huck Finn,’ Dies at 84
item.description: He made it his mission to track down every book Mark Twain owned — and to fix what he saw as flaws that kept schools from teaching the author’s most famous works.
item.dc:creator: Michael S. Rosenwald

item.title: Caissie Levy, Tony Awards Contender and ‘Ragtime’ Star, Is Having a Moment
item.description: Caissie Levy was Broadway’s first Elsa. She starred in “Hair” and “Ghost.” And now, for “Ragtime,” she is an odds-on favorite to win a Tony Award.
item.dc:creator: Michael Paulson and George Etheredge
item.media:credit: George Etheredge for The New York Times

item.title: Book Review: ‘The Traveler,’ by Andrea Wulf
item.description: As presented in Andrea Wulf’s new biography, “The Traveler,” George Forster was an impressively curious, open-minded 17-year-old naturalist and polymath.
item.dc:creator: Jennifer Szalai
item.category: The Traveler: One Man's Quest for Humanity From the South Seas to Revolutionary Paris (Book)

item.title: Carley Fortune Keeps Writing Hit Romance Novels
item.description: Carley Fortune left a hard-won journalism job to give fiction a shot. Five best-sellers later, a series based on her debut is about to stream.
item.dc:creator: Elisabeth Egan
item.media:credit: Narisa Ladak for The New York Times

item.title: How Poets Went From Describing Art to Personally Admiring It
item.description: The form known as ekphrasis — or poetry about art — has taken a turn toward the individual. Our columnist asks what it means.
item.dc:creator: Elisa Gabbert

item.title: Book Review: ‘The Wreck of the Mentor,’ by Eric Jay Dolin
item.description: In “The Wreck of the Mentor,” the maritime historian Eric Jay Dolin brings to life a dramatic episode from the golden age of whaling.
item.dc:creator: Blair Braverman
item.category: The Wreck of the Mentor: A True Story of Death, Despair, and Deliverance in the Age of Sail (Book)

item.title: In Her Memoir, Jill Biden Is a Watchful Spouse Who Didn’t Always Speak Up About Joe Biden
item.description: The former first lady’s new book reflects an insular White House where loyalty was prized and President Joseph R. Biden Jr.’s feelings were prioritized over health concerns.
item.dc:creator: Katie Rogers
item.category: View from the East Wing: A Memoir (Book)
item.media:description: Jill Biden in 2022. In her telling, by the time she got a glimpse of what others had feared about her husband as president, it was too late.

item.title: Book Review: ‘What Came West,’ by Josh Weil
item.description: Josh Weil’s new novel follows an autistic trapper on an odyssey during the California gold rush.
item.dc:creator: Hamilton Cain

item.title: Book Review: ‘Land,’ by Maggie O’Farrell
item.description: Set in the decades after the Great Hunger, “Land” is a rich portrait of family life amid Ireland’s long struggle against British rule.
item.dc:creator: Fiona Mozley

item.title: Book Review: ‘Stolen Revolution,’ by Yeganeh Torbati and Bozorgmehr Sharafedin
item.description: In a quietly devastating new book, two journalists chart the protest movements fighting for change inside the country.
item.dc:creator: Reza Aslan
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
Stolen Revolution: Betrayal and Hope in Modern Iran (Book)
item.media:description: Iranians gather on Tehran’s Shah Reza Avenue, now called Enqelab (“Revolution”) Street, in 1979.

item.title: Book Review: ‘The Fire Agent,’ by David Baerwald
item.description: “The Fire Agent,” by David Baerwald, is a historical novel that spans two continents and world wars.
item.dc:creator: Dan Fesperman

item.title: Book Review: ‘The Man Who Read Everything: The Literary Letters of Harold Bloom’
item.description: A collection of Harold Bloom’s letters details the working life of one of America’s most influential intellects.
item.dc:creator: Dwight Garner
item.category: The Man Who Read Everything: The Literary Letters of Harold Bloom (Book)
item.media:description: “Lord knows what I am,” Harold Bloom wrote in 1975. “Not a poet, critic, scholar, historian, psychoanalyst, philosopher or theosophist — but a worthless mélange of the gang of it.”

item.title: 9 Comic Books and Graphic Novels to Celebrate Pride Month
item.description: Historical chronicles and flights of fancy, all with L.G.B.T.Q. protagonists, arrive starting in June.
item.dc:creator: George Gene Gustines
item.category: Wiccan and Hulkling: Raid of Ultron (Book)
Justice League: Dream Girls A DC Pride Event (Book)
Bitter Pill: Randy Shilts and the Dawn of the HIV/AIDS Epidemic (Book)

item.title: 28 New Books to Read in June: Ann Patchett, Maggie O’Farrell, Daniel Kraus and More
item.description: Novels by Ann Patchett, Maggie O’Farrell and Dave Eggers; memoirs by Jill Biden and Laverne Cox; sci-fi adventures by a Pulitzer Prize winner; and more.

item.title: Book Review: ‘1873,’ by Liaquat Ahamed
item.description: In “1873,” the historian and financier Liaquat Ahamed traces the political consequences of booming markets that left a lot of people behind.
item.dc:creator: Trevor Jackson
item.category: 1873: The Rothschilds, the First Great Depression, and the Making of the Modern World (Book)

item.title: Joana Avillez Revisits Her Waterfront Childhood
item.description: Joana Avillez took six years to illustrate a new edition of Joseph Mitchell’s “The Bottom of the Harbor,” which captures the salty New York neighborhood of her youth.
item.dc:creator: Alex Vadukul
item.media:credit: Elias Williams for The New York Times
item.media:description: Joana Avillez poses for a portrait not far from the building where she grew up, near the South Street Seaport in Lower Manhattan.

item.title: Book Review: ‘Rabbit, Fox, Tar,’ by P.C. Verrone
item.description: In “Rabbit, Fox, Tar,” a white neighborhood’s local election is complicated when a mysterious, dark-skinned woman suddenly appears in town.
item.dc:creator: Mateo Askaripour

item.title: The Month’s Best New Mystery Novels
item.description: Our critic on four terrific new mysteries.
item.dc:creator: Sarah Weinman

item.title: Book Review: ‘Girl’s Girl,’ by Sonia Feldman
item.description: In Sonia Feldman’s novel, “Girl’s Girl,” the delicate balance of a Gen Z friend group is unsettled over one Ohio summer.
item.dc:creator: Sanjena Sathian

item.title: Book Review: ‘Sublimation,’ by Isabel J. Kim
item.description: In her book “Sublimation,” Isabel J. Kim reimagines the dilemmas of immigration through a science fiction story about scheming clones.
item.dc:creator: Daniel Nieh

item.title: Book Club: Let’s Talk About ‘Transcription,’ by Ben Lerner
item.description: Lerner’s new novel is a cerebral exploration of technology, family, truth and existence.
item.media:credit: Photo Illustration by The New York Times; Cover by Farrar, Straus and Giroux

item.title: Book Club: Read ‘Yesteryear,’ by Caro Claire Burke, With the Book Review
item.description: In June, the Book Review Book Club will read and discuss a novel about a tradwife who wakes up in 1855, living the pioneer life she has been performing online.
item.dc:creator: MJ Franklin

item.title: Book Review: ‘View From the East Wing,’ by Jill Biden
item.description: Beyond a few pointed digs at her husband’s successor, “View From the East Wing” largely sticks to the head-spinning details of first lady-hood.
item.dc:creator: Alexandra Jacobs
item.category: View from the East Wing: A Memoir (Book)
item.media:description: Jill Biden returning to the White House in January 2025, weeks before her husband finished his term as president.

item.title: Book Review: ‘Found Sound,’ by Meg Wolitzer and Charlie Panek, and ‘Stream,’ by Aida Salazar
item.description: Meg Wolitzer and Charlie Panek’s “Found Sound” and Aida Salazar’s “Stream” send their protagonists on a listening (and healing) tour of real life.
item.dc:creator: Jennifer Howard
